content.full_text:
August 19, 1976

76-08-228

Assemblywoman Pauline Davis
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Assemblywoman Davis:

Last week you asked me to provide you with informal
advice on the question of your reporting responsibilities
for a dinner held in your honor. Tickets to the dinner
are $6.00 and it is expected that the proceeds will be used
for your trip to Europe.

The event must be reported either on your campaign
statement or your Statement of Economic Interests. In order
to determine whether to report the dinner on your Statement
of Economic Interest or campaign statement, it is necessary
to apply the provisions of the Political Reform Act and the
regulations adopted by the Commission to the facts provided
by you.

A campaign statement must report contributions received
or expenditures made. A payment received by you is a contribution "unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances
that it [the payment] is not made for political purposes."
Government Code Section 82013.

In a regulation the Commission has defined "political
purposes" to mean "for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure." 2 Cal. Adm. Code
18215(a).

If the payment is a contribution, your campaign statement
must itemize each contribution of $50 or more and each expenditure of $50 or more. In determining the $50 threshold, you
must list those persons who purchase ten or more $6.00 tickets
or persons who make payments of $50 or more; for such persons,

13

Assemblywoman Pauline Davis
Page Two                                August 19, 1976


you must provide the address, occupation, and employer.
Finally, no lobbyist may purchase a ticket to the event
since the lobbyist would be making a political contribution.
Employers of lobbyists are permitted to buy tickets pro-
vided that the lobbyist does not arrange the contribution.

     It is my understanding from our conversation that all of
the dinner's proceeds will be used for a gift to you and none
will be used for any political purposes such as paying off
past debts, making contributions to other candidates, etc.
If the above is correct, then the payments received by you
are not contributions but gifts.

     Under the Political Reform Act, the source of any gift
of $25 or more received must be reported on your Statement of
Economic Interest. Gift is defined as "any payment to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received." Government Code Section 92028.

     The source of the gift to you will be each person who
buys a ticket to the event. Thus you must list on your State-
ment of Economic Interest (Form 721, Schedule E) each person
who purchases five or more tickets or any person who makes
payments of $25 or more. Unlike the reporting requirements
for campaign statements, you need not list the person's
occupation or employer's name. Also you do not have to
itemize expenditures.

     A lobbyist may not purchase more than one $6.00 ticket
because of Government Code Section 86203 which prohibits a
lobbyist from making a gift of more than $10 per month to a
state officeholder.

     You have also asked about the reporting requirements of
persons who purchase tickets. Lobbyists must report the pur-
chase of even one ticket. Employers of lobbyists and persons
who spend $250 or more in any month to influence legislative
or administrative action must report any payment of $25 or
more. All other persons do not have any reporting requirements.

     In conclusion, it is my informal view that you should
report the dinner on your Statement of Economic Interest since
it appears that it is not being held for political purposes.

Assemblywoman Pauline Davis
Page 3                          August 19, 1976




     Please feel free to call on me if you have any further
questions.

                                    Very truly yours,


                                    Robert M. Stern
                                    General Counsel

RMS:nc

sections.conclusion: In conclusion, it is my informal view that you should report the dinner on your Statement of Economic Interest since it appears that it is not being held for political purposes.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the reporting requirements for an Assemblywoman regarding a fundraising dinner held in her honor, where $6 tickets will generate proceeds for her personal trip to Europe?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the dinner proceeds should be reported on the Statement of Economic Interest (Form 721, Schedule E) rather than a campaign statement, since the funds are for personal use (a gift) rather than political purposes. The Assemblywoman must list each person purchasing five or more tickets or making payments of $25 or more.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains clear question and conclusion in body text. Dated August 19, 1976, signed by Robert M. Stern, General Counsel. Comprehensive analysis of Political Reform Act provisions and FPPC regulations (2 Cal. Adm. Code 18215(a), Gov. Code Sections 82013, 82028, and 86203).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the reporting requirements for an Assemblywoman regarding a fundraising dinner held in her honor, where $6 tickets will generate proceeds for her personal trip to Europe?

CONCLUSION: In conclusion, it is my informal view that you should report the dinner on your Statement of Economic Interest since it appears that it is not being held for political purposes.

embedding.summary: FPPC General Counsel provides informal advice to Assemblywoman Pauline Davis regarding reporting requirements for a $6/ticket fundraising dinner benefiting her personal trip to Europe. The advice distinguishes between contributions (requiring campaign statement reporting) and gifts (requiring Statement of Economic Interest reporting), and outlines specific thresholds and restrictions for ticket purchases by lobbyists and others.
